title: French Revolution Begins
summary: The storming of the Bastille on July 14, 1789 marked the opening of the French Revolutionary period — the point at which political crisis became open insurrection. What followed was not a single event but a decade of radical transformation: the National Assembly legislated a new France, the monarchy was abolished, thousands were guillotined in the Terror, and Napoleon eventually emerged from the wreckage to spread revolutionary ideals by force across Europe. The Revolution's causes were long in building; its consequences are still felt today.

title: Ancien Régime Structure
summary: French society was legally divided into three estates: the First Estate (clergy), Second Estate (nobility), and Third Estate (everyone else — roughly 97% of the population). The First and Second Estates were largely exempt from taxation while enjoying extensive legal privileges. This arrangement had persisted largely unchanged since the medieval period, making structural reform almost impossible without the consent of those who benefited from it.

title: Estates System Inequality
summary: The clergy and nobility together made up roughly 3% of France's population yet owned approximately 35% of the land and paid almost no direct taxes. The Third Estate — from wealthy bourgeois merchants to destitute peasants — bore nearly the entire tax burden. This stark structural inequality had been tolerated for generations, but by the 1780s, with food prices rising and the treasury collapsing, it became politically explosive.

title: Tax Burden on Commoners
summary: French commoners paid the taille (a direct land tax), the gabelle (salt tax), road-building corvée labour obligations, and Church tithes — while nobles and clergy were exempt from most of these. The tax system was also administered by private tax farmers who kept a share of what they collected, making it both inefficient and deeply resented as legalised extortion.

title: Growing Public Resentment
summary: Decades of visible inequality — nobles exempt from taxes while peasants starved, the Church accumulating wealth while tithe collectors visited even the poorest villages — built a reservoir of anger that required only a trigger to become politically explosive. By the late 1780s, broadsheets, pamphlets, and Enlightenment philosophy had given this resentment a vocabulary and a target.

title: Enlightenment Ideas Spread
summary: Voltaire attacked the Church and arbitrary royal power. Montesquieu argued for separation of powers. Rousseau developed the concept of popular sovereignty — the idea that legitimate government requires the consent of the governed. These ideas circulated through salons, pamphlets, and the Encyclopédie, giving educated French citizens a framework for imagining a radically different political order.

title: Questioning Royal Authority
summary: The doctrine of divine right — that kings ruled by God's direct mandate and answered to no earthly authority — had justified absolute monarchy for centuries. By the 1780s, Enlightenment arguments had made this claim intellectually untenable for much of the educated public. Political authority came to be seen as a contract between ruler and ruled, not a gift from heaven — a shift that made Louis XVI's failures feel like breaches of obligation rather than God's will.

title: Monarchy Financial Crisis
summary: By 1788, France was effectively bankrupt. Debt service consumed roughly 60% of royal revenue — the result of decades of war spending and a tax system incapable of reaching the wealthiest subjects. The government had already borrowed to its limits, and international creditors were losing confidence. Without fundamental fiscal reform, the monarchy could not function.

title: War Debt Accumulation
summary: France's support for the American Revolution (1778–1783) and its costly defeat in the Seven Years' War (1756–1763) together added catastrophically to the royal debt. The American venture alone cost roughly 1.3 billion livres. The ideological irony was acute: France had helped fund a revolution based on principles that its own people would soon turn against the French crown.

title: Inefficient Tax System
summary: France's tax system was a patchwork of medieval exemptions, regional variations, and noble privileges that made it structurally incapable of raising sufficient revenue. Repeated attempts at reform — including Louis XVI's Assembly of Notables in 1787 — were blocked by the privileged classes. The nobility's ability to veto its own taxation made the fiscal crisis insoluble through normal channels.

title: Failed Tax Reforms
summary: Finance Minister Calonne proposed taxing all landowners regardless of rank in 1786 — a radical break from tradition that the Assembly of Notables rejected outright. Each failed attempt at reform narrowed the monarchy's options further: the privileged classes could veto any plan that threatened their exemptions, while the treasury hemorrhaged. It was Necker's earlier publication of the royal accounts in the Compte rendu (1781, during his first ministry) that had already made the scale of the fiscal crisis public knowledge — making every subsequent failure more politically charged. By 1788, only a meeting of the Estates-General remained as an option, and even that was an admission of defeat.

title: Estates-General Convened
summary: Louis XVI convened the Estates-General in May 1789 — the first time it had met since 1614. The convening immediately produced a constitutional crisis: the Third Estate demanded that votes be counted by head rather than by estate (which would give them a majority), while the nobility insisted on the traditional arrangement where each estate had one vote regardless of size. This procedural dispute became the revolution's first flashpoint.

title: Third Estate Asserts Power
summary: Locked out of the Estates-General meeting hall in June 1789, the Third Estate's deputies found the doors literally barred. They reconvened at a nearby tennis court and declared themselves the National Assembly — claiming the right to speak for the French nation as a whole. Their refusal to disperse even under royal command was the first direct constitutional challenge to Louis XVI's authority.

title: Tennis Court Oath
summary: On June 20, 1789, members of the Third Estate — joined by sympathetic clergy — swore not to disperse until France had a written constitution. The oath transformed a tax dispute into a constitutional revolution: for the first time, elected representatives were claiming sovereign authority in direct defiance of the king. Almost every one of the 577 deputies signed.

title: National Assembly Formed
summary: The Third Estate's deputies, joined by reform-minded clergy and some nobles, declared themselves the National Assembly of France on June 17, 1789 — asserting that sovereignty resided in the nation, not the crown. When Louis XVI attempted to dissolve them, the deputies refused. Within weeks he was forced to formally recognise the Assembly, effectively ending absolute monarchy.

title: Bread Shortages Crisis
summary: The harvest failure of 1788 caused bread prices to reach their highest level in decades by the spring of 1789. In Paris, a four-pound loaf of bread cost nearly a full day's wages for a labourer. For urban workers spending 80–90% of their income on food, this was not hardship but starvation. Hunger transformed political discontent into physical desperation.

title: Financial Strain on People
summary: Years of high taxation and poor harvests had left French peasants and urban workers with almost no financial cushion. Even before 1788's catastrophic harvest, many rural families were chronically indebted to landlords and tax collectors. The additional shock of food price inflation in 1789 pushed millions from poverty into genuine destitution, making revolutionary action feel like survival rather than politics.

title: Urban Unrest
summary: In the spring and early summer of 1789, grain convoys were attacked across France, markets were invaded, and local officials were threatened. In Paris, crowds gathered around the Palais-Royal to hear radical speeches. The dismissal of finance minister Necker on July 11 transformed simmering unrest into open insurrection within 48 hours.

title: Storming of Bastille
summary: On July 14, 1789, Parisian crowds stormed the Bastille fortress, killing its governor and releasing its seven prisoners. The fortress held almost no one and was militarily irrelevant — but it symbolised royal tyranny and the possibility of defeating it. News spread across France within days, triggering the 'Great Fear' as peasants attacked noble estates throughout the countryside.

title: Monarchy Abolished
summary: On September 21, 1792, the newly elected National Convention abolished the monarchy by unanimous vote and declared France a republic. The decision came one day after the French revolutionary army's unexpected victory at Valmy — the first sign that the republic could defend itself militarily. Louis XVI was placed on trial for treason the following month.

title: Execution of Louis XVI
summary: Louis XVI was guillotined on January 21, 1793, before a large crowd in the Place de la Révolution. His execution horrified European monarchies, accelerating the formation of the First Coalition against France and giving the war an ideological dimension it had previously lacked. Within France, it committed the revolution irrevocably: there was now no monarchy to restore, no going back.

title: Reign of Terror
summary: Between September 1793 and July 1794, the Committee of Public Safety under Maximilien Robespierre executed approximately 17,000 people officially and perhaps 40,000 in total, including former revolutionaries, nobles, priests, and ordinary citizens denounced by neighbours. The Terror was justified as a wartime emergency measure to eliminate counter-revolutionary threats — but it consumed its own architects when Robespierre was arrested on 9 Thermidor (July 27, 1794) and guillotined the following day.

title: Political Radicalization
summary: As the revolution faced foreign invasion and internal rebellion, moderate constitutional monarchists gave way to Girondins, who in turn were purged by Jacobins. Each escalation was driven by genuine crisis — war, food shortages, conspiracy — but also by the revolutionary logic that any compromise with enemies was treason. By 1793 the political centre had been destroyed, leaving only radical factions competing to prove their revolutionary purity.

title: Seven Years' War
summary: The Seven Years' War (1756–1763) was effectively the first world war, fought across Europe, North America, India, and the Caribbean. France lost Canada, its Indian trading posts, and much of its colonial empire to Britain, while accumulating enormous debt. The humiliating defeat damaged royal prestige and left France determined to reverse British dominance — a goal that led directly to its ruinously expensive support for American independence.

title: France Backs American Revolution
summary: France intervened in the American Revolutionary War from 1778, driven by the desire to weaken Britain after the Seven Years' War humiliation. The strategy succeeded militarily — American independence was secured — but at catastrophic financial cost, adding roughly 1.3 billion livres to the royal debt. The revolutionary ideals France helped export soon returned home, now armed with an American example of successful republican government.

title: Louis XVI's Weak Leadership
summary: Louis XVI was by temperament a man suited to administration in stable times — conscientious, well-meaning, and genuinely interested in reform — but catastrophically ill-equipped for revolutionary crisis. He repeatedly agreed to reforms under pressure and then reversed course when the pressure eased, destroying his credibility with all factions. His most consequential failure was his inability to choose between genuine constitutionalism and counter-revolution; his attempts at both convinced everyone he was committed to neither.

title: Necker Dismissed
summary: Louis XVI dismissed the popular finance minister Jacques Necker on July 11, 1789 — just three days before the Bastille fell. Parisians interpreted the dismissal as a signal that the king was planning to use the troops massing around Paris to dissolve the National Assembly by force. The news spread through the city within hours, and crowds began arming themselves that same afternoon.

title: Harvest Failure of 1788
summary: A severe drought followed by a brutal winter destroyed harvests across France. Bread prices soared to unaffordable levels just as the political crisis peaked — hunger became a revolutionary force.

relatedScenario.label: Connects to Year Without a Summer

title: Women's March on Versailles
summary: On October 5, 1789, thousands of Parisian market women marched 21 kilometres to Versailles demanding bread and an end to the food crisis. They invaded the palace, killed two royal guards, and forced the royal family to return to Paris the following day. The king and his family were now physically in the capital, under the effective supervision of the revolutionary crowd — prisoners in all but name. Louis XVI's subsequent experience of confinement in Paris directly shaped his decision to attempt the disastrous escape to Varennes in June 1791.

title: Declaration of Rights of Man
summary: Adopted by the National Assembly on August 26, 1789, the Declaration proclaimed that liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression were natural and inalienable rights of all men. Drafted partly with reference to the American Declaration of Independence, it became the foundational text of modern liberal democracy, directly inspiring revolutionary movements across Latin America, Europe, and beyond throughout the 19th century.

title: Flight to Varennes
summary: In June 1791, Louis XVI and his family disguised themselves as servants and attempted to flee to the Austrian border, where Queen Marie Antoinette's nephew Emperor Leopold II had troops. They were recognised and arrested at Varennes, 50 kilometres from safety. The escape attempt was politically catastrophic: it confirmed suspicions that Louis was secretly negotiating with foreign powers to crush the revolution, and destroyed what remained of his public credibility.

title: European Coalitions Against France
summary: Austria and Prussia issued the Declaration of Pillnitz in 1791, warning that they would intervene if Louis XVI was threatened. France declared war on Austria in April 1792; within months Prussia, Britain, Spain, and the Netherlands joined the coalition. The threat of foreign invasion unified French opinion behind the revolution and radicalised it simultaneously — war emergency became the justification for the Terror.

title: Thermidorian Reaction
summary: On 9 Thermidor Year II (July 27, 1794), members of the National Convention voted to arrest Robespierre, Saint-Just, and their allies, who were guillotined the following day. The Thermidorian Reaction that followed systematically dismantled the machinery of the Terror: the Committee of Public Safety was stripped of power, the Jacobin Club was closed, and surviving political prisoners were released. France pulled back from its most extreme phase, exhausted and traumatised.

title: The Directory
summary: A five-man executive Directory governed France from November 1795 to November 1799. It was constitutionally designed to prevent any single leader from accumulating too much power — and succeeded only in preventing effective governance. Plagued by corruption, factional intrigue, and military dependence, the Directory staged two coups against its own elected legislators to survive. By 1799 it was universally despised, making Napoleon's coup almost welcome.

title: Napoleon Seizes Power
summary: Napoleon Bonaparte staged the coup of 18 Brumaire (November 9, 1799) with the support of key Directory members and his brother Lucien, who presided over the legislature. He became First Consul — in practice sole ruler — ending the revolutionary period. He preserved some revolutionary gains: the Civil Code enshrined legal equality, meritocracy replaced birth privilege in state appointments, and Church-state relations were rationalised. But he also restored authoritarian power, censored the press, and built a police state. The Directory's universal unpopularity made resistance minimal; most French citizens were simply relieved the chaos had ended.

relatedScenario.label: Connects to The Sale That Made America

title: Napoleonic Wars
summary: Napoleon's campaigns spread revolutionary legal codes across Europe while reshaping the continent's borders. The wars also exhausted France and eventually produced the Concert of Europe that would dominate the 19th century.

title: Revolutionary Ideas Spread
summary: The French Revolution's core ideas — that sovereignty derives from the people, that hereditary privilege is illegitimate, that nations have the right to self-determination — proved impossible to contain. They inspired the Haitian Revolution (1791), Latin American independence movements, the liberal revolutions of 1830 and 1848 across Europe, and the unification movements of Italy and Germany. The French Revolution did not merely change France; it changed the political vocabulary of the modern world.

title: Feudalism Abolished in France
summary: In a dramatic overnight session on August 4–5, 1789, the National Assembly voted to abolish feudal privileges entirely. Noble after noble rose to renounce their rights — hunting monopolies, mill fees, serfdom obligations, Church tithes — in a competitive cascade of generosity that transformed the legal structure of French society in a single night. Centuries of inherited obligation were legally extinguished before dawn.
